coredata.dc:title: Enriched graphitic N dopants of carbon dots as F cores mediate photothermal conversion in the NIR-II window with high efficiency
coredata.dc:creator: Geng, Bijiang
Shen, Wenwen
Fang, Fuling
Qin, Hua
Li, Ping
Wang, Xulong
Li, Xiaokai
Pan, Dengyu
Shen, Longxiang
coredata.dc:description: We have developed the rapid synthesis of NIR–II–responsive carbon dots (NIR–II–CDs) under microwave irradiation conditions. A close correlation between the photothermal effects and graphitic N doping level is well established by two observed effects: 1) absorbance at 808 and 1064 nm was consistently proportional to the graphitic N content; 2) the photothermal conversion efficiency remarkably increased with the graphitic N content. We further revealed a distinct photothermal conversion mechanism in the NIR–II–CDs, where doped graphitic N sites as paramagnetic F centers can mediate the highly efficient NIR absorption and photothermal conversion. Importantly, readily derived NIR–II–CDs with a graphitic N doping level as high as 4.30 at.% showed ultrahigh photothermal conversion efficiency of 81.3% in the NIR-II window. Using NIR–II–CDs or their hybrid liposomal formula as single PTT or imaging-guided PCT agents, complete tumor ablation without recurrence was realized under the 1064-nm laser irradiation at low laser power densities even in the presence of the breast tissue with a thickness larger than 10 mm. Our results suggest that the photothermal properties of biocompatible CDs can be regulated in the extended NIR regions by doping a high concentration of graphitic N defects as color centers for tumor treatment with minimized side effects.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Ultrafast microwave synthesis of NIR–II–CDs doped with graphitic N and functionalized with pyrrolic N and hydroxyl from microwave-responsive precursors TNP, BPEI, and H2O. (b) Optimizing the microwave synthetic procedure of NIR–II–CDs by monitoring the 1064-nm NIR absorbance with varied TNP concentration, mass ratio of BPEI to TNP, reaction temperature, and reaction time. The optimized sample, NIR–II–CD-4.30%, was prepared under the dashed line marked reaction conditions. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Structural characterization of NIR–II–CD-4.30%. (a) Survey XPS spectrum. (b) High-resolution C 1s spectrum. (c) High-resolution N 1s spectrum. (d) High-resolution O 1s spectrum. (e) TEM image (inset: lateral size distribution). (f) HRTEM image. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Photothermal performance of NIR–II–CD-4.30% solutions (500 μg mL−1) in the NIR-I and NIR-II windows. (a) Optical absorption spectrum in the NIR region from 650 to 1300 nm (Inset: photograph of the solution). (b) Infrared thermographic images of NIR–II–CD-4.30% solutions filled in centrifuge tubes under continuous 808 and 1064 nm laser irradiation from 0 to 5 min. (c, d) Time-dependent temperature elevation of the NIR–II–CD-4.30% solution exposed to the 808 nm laser (c) and 1064 nm laser (d) at power densities lower than that of MPE limit (0.42 W cm−2 at 808 nm; 1.0 W cm−2 at 1064 nm). (e) Photothermal effect of the solution exposed to the 808 nm laser at 0.3 W cm−2 and to the 1064 nm laser at 0.6 W cm−2. The lasers were shut off after 300 s irradiation. (f) Plot of cooling time versus negative natural logarithm of the temperature driving force obtained from the cooling period after the 808 nm and 1064 nm irradiation. The time constant τs for heat transfer of the system was determined to be 204.4 s and 141.8 s, respectively. (g, h) Temperature variation of the NIR–II–CD-4.30% solution over several on/off irradiation cycles. (i–l) Evaluation of the tissue-thickness-dependent photothermal effect of the NIR–II–CD-4.30% solution after the NIR lasers passed through chicken breast tissue of various thicknesses (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 mm) for 808 nm (i) and 1064 nm (k). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) NIR absorption spectra of three NIR–II–CD samples at the same concentration (500 μg mL−1). (b) The absorbance data at 808 nm and 1064 nm (derived from (a)) as a function of graphitic N content. The data were linearly fitted (R2 = 0.996–0.998). (c) Photothermal effect of the NIR–II–CD samples exposed to the 1064 nm laser at 0.6 W cm−2. The lasers were shut off after 300 s irradiation. (d) Table lists the structural parameters and the photothermal conversion efficiency of NIR–II–CD samples. (e) EPR signals of NIR–II–CD powders measured at room temperature. (f) Proposed formation mechanism of F centers at graphitic N sites, where N atoms donate one excess electron and form N+ cations to trap the electron with a large binding energy. (g) Defect energy levels of doped graphitic N as a F center within the wide band gap of NIR–II–CDs and possible optical transitions from the HOMO level to the defect level and from the singly-occupied defect level to the LUMO level under the NIR excitation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) PL spectra of NIR–II–CD-4.30% excited at different wavelengths varied from 370 to 450 nm. Inset: photograph of NIR–II–CD-4.30% solutions under a UV light. (b) Confocal image of HeLa cells incubated with NIR–II–CD-4.30%. (c) Cytotoxicity essay of the NIR–II–CD-4.30% against HeLa cells after incubation for 24 h and 48 h without irradiation and with irradiation under the 1064 nm laser at power density of 0.8 W cm−2 for 5 min. (d) Schematic illustration of deep tissue PTT. Nude mice bearing xenograft 4T1 tumors were intratumorally injected with NIR–II–CD (100 μL, 750 μg mL−1) or saline (100 μL), respectively. A piece of 10 mm chicken breast tissue was placed on top of the tumor during laser irradiation. Power density: 1064 nm, 1.0 W cm−2; 808 nm, 0.4 W cm−2. (e) Tumor growth curves of mice after intratumoral administration of NIR–II–CD or saline with and without irradiation of 808 or 1064 nm laser. (f) Quantitative analysis of pulmonary metastatic nodules for every group after treatment. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Schematic of the preparation procedure of NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL (TSL: PEGylated thermosensitive liposome). (b, c) TEM images of NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL. (d) DLS spectrum of NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL. (e) UV–vis–NIR absorption spectra of free NIR–II–CD, NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL and NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL. (f) DOX release from NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL as a function of temperature. (g) Time course of DOX release from NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL at various temperatures. (h) Confocal fluorescence images of HeLa cells incubated with NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL at 37 °C and 42 °C. (i) Infrared thermographic images of NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL solutions filled in centrifuge tubes under continuous 1064 nm laser irradiation at the power density of 0.8 and 0.6 W cm−2, respectively. (j) In vivo fluorescence imaging of 4T1 tumor-bearing Balb/c nude mice at different time points following an intravenous injection of NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL. Red circles indicate the position of the implanted tumor. (k) Ex vivo images of isolated organs and tumors of mice at different time points after intravenous injection of NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL. The color bars represent the fluorescence intensity. (l) Maximum temperature within the tumor region recorded by an IR camera after systematic administration and 1064 nm laser irradiation at the power density of 0.8 and 0.6 W cm−2, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Schematic illustration of deep tissue PCT. Nude mice bearing xenograft 4T1 tumors were intravenously injected with NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL (200 μL, 500 μg mL−1). A piece of 10 mm chicken breast tissue was placed on top of the tumor during laser irradiation. Power density: 1064 nm, 1.0 W cm−2. (b) Tumor growth curves of mice after receiving different treatments via intravenous injection including saline, saline + laser, NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL, and NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL + 808 nm laser irradiation (0.3 W cm−2). (c) Tumor growth curves of mice after receiving different treatments via intravenous injection including saline, free DOX, NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL + 1064 nm laser irradiation (0.8 W cm−2), NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL + 1064 nm laser irradiation (0.6 W cm−2), and NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL + tissue + 1064 nm laser irradiation (1.0 W cm−2). (d) Quantitative analysis of pulmonary metastatic nodules for every group after treatment. (e) Monitoring curves of the body weight of mice over the whole treatment period. (f) Pictures of tumors obtained from mice on day 18 after receiving different treatments. (g) Representative photographs of mice bearing 4T1 tumors on 18 days after different treatments. The tumor was marked with red circles. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The whole blood and major organs including heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney were collected from the Balb/c mice that were sacrificed at 18 days after intravenously injected of saline (group 1), NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL (group 2), free DOX (group 3), saline+1064 (0.8 W cm−2) (group 4), NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL+808 (0.3 W cm−2) (group 5), NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL+1064 (0.8 W cm−2) (group 6), and NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL+1064 (0.6 W cm−2) (group 7), respectively. (a) white blood cells (WBC), red blood cells (RBC), mean corpuscular volume (MCV), hemoglobin (HGB), platelet (PLT), and Mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH). (b) H&E staining images. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Comparison of NIR–II–CDs and other kinds of PTT agents in the photothermal conversion efficiency.

head.ce:title: Enriched graphitic N dopants of carbon dots as F cores mediate photothermal conversion in the NIR-II window with high efficiency

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We have developed the rapid synthesis of NIR–II–responsive carbon dots (NIR–II–CDs) under microwave irradiation conditions. A close correlation between the photothermal effects and graphitic N doping level is well established by two observed effects: 1) absorbance at 808 and 1064 nm was consistently proportional to the graphitic N content; 2) the photothermal conversion efficiency remarkably increased with the graphitic N content. We further revealed a distinct photothermal conversion mechanism in the NIR–II–CDs, where doped graphitic N sites as paramagnetic F centers can mediate the highly efficient NIR absorption and photothermal conversion. Importantly, readily derived NIR–II–CDs with a graphitic N doping level as high as 4.30 at.% showed ultrahigh photothermal conversion efficiency of 81.3% in the NIR-II window. Using NIR–II–CDs or their hybrid liposomal formula as single PTT or imaging-guided PCT agents, complete tumor ablation without recurrence was realized under the 1064-nm laser irradiation at low laser power densities even in the presence of the breast tissue with a thickness larger than 10 mm. Our results suggest that the photothermal properties of biocompatible CDs can be regulated in the extended NIR regions by doping a high concentration of graphitic N defects as color centers for tumor treatment with minimized side effects.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, continued efforts have been focused on the development of photothermal agents that hold promise in solid tumor ablation with minimized side effects through local hyperpyrexia in tumors induced by near-infrared (NIR) irradiation [1–5]. It is desirable to synthesize photothermal agents with excellent photostability and biocompatibility that are able to operate in the most suitable biological window, the known NIR-II region (1000–1350 nm), for photothermal (PTT) or photothermal-chemo combination (PCT) therapy of tumors in deep tissues. Unfortunately, PTT agents synthesized so far have shown photothermal effects commonly confined in the inefficient and unsafe NIR-I window (700–950 nm) [6–8], in which strong skin scattering and tissue absorption occur under laser irradiation at rather high power densities required. To expand the NIR response to the NIR-II region, various nanostructures of noble metals [9,10], metal oxides [11,12], metal sulfides [13,14], carbon nanotubes [15,16], and polymers [17–19] have been developed. However, their applications are limited by low photothermal conversion efficiency (ƞ) in the NIR-II window, leading to incomplete tumor ablation at or lower than the maximum permission exposure (1.0 W cm−2). Recently, the record 80.8% conversion efficiency of PTT agents in the NIR-II window have been achieved in Au plasmonic coupling among branches [10], but the long-term toxicity of heavy metals as well as the low photostability and high cost has limited in vivo biomedical applications.

As metal-free quantum dots with unique physiochemical properties, carbon dots (CDs) have aroused intensive interest in a broad range of fields, including bioimaging [20–22], biosensing [23,24], drug delivery [25,26], and photodynamic therapy [27,28]. Recently, NIR-responsive CDs (NIR-CDs) as PTT agents with excellent optical stability have been developed by bottom-up methods using costly organic materials (polythiophene [30], polythiophene phenylpropionic acid [31], or CyOH dye [32]). Albeit remarkable advantages over previously reported PTT agents, NIR-CDs with distinct photothermal properties extended into the beneficial NIR-II window have not been synthesized for PTT or PCT applications.

Herein, we report for the first time the synthesis of novel NIR-responsive CDs (NIR–II–CDs) in the NIR-II window for deep-tissue tumor treatment. By a rapid microwave-assisting reaction, a high doping level of graphitic N in CDs was successfully realized, enabling the photothermal conversion efficiency at 1064 nm to be dramatically increased to 81.3%, compared to the record reported so far (80.8% in Au [10]). To elucidate the graphitic N-induced extraordinary photothermal effects of NIR–II–CDs, we observed that the absorbance at 1064 nm was proportional to the graphitic N content, and the photothermal conversion efficiency remarkably increased from 43.6% at lower graphitic N (1.68 at.%) to 81.3% at a higher graphitic N (4.30 at.%). Further studies reveal that graphitic N sites as paramagnetic N “color centers” doped in the wide bandgap semiconductor may induce single-occupied middle states in the bandgap for optical transitions in the NIR-II region. Finally, their applications for tumor ablation in deeper tissue were evaluated in single PTT or imaging-guided PCT. Complete tumor ablation without recurrence was realized under the 1064-nm laser irradiation at low laser power densities even in the presence of the breast tissue with a thickness larger than 10 mm.

Pyrene (purity>98%) and indocyanine green (ICG) were obtained from TCI (Japan). Polymers of branched polyethylenimine (BPEI, 10,000 Da) was obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). 1,2-Dipalmitoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (DPPC), Hydrogenated-l-α-phosphatidylcholine (HSPC), 1,2-distearoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine-N-PEG2000 (DSPE-PEG2000) and cholesterol were obtained from A.V.T (Shanghai) Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM), roswell park memorial institute-1640 (RPMI 1640), heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS), and trypsin were purchased from Gibco (USA).

1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) was first prepared through the nitration reaction of pyrene (purity>98%) following our previous report [34]. Then, the NIR–II–responsive CDs (NIR–II–CDs) were synthesized in a microwave instrument (Anton Paar Monowave 400) using TNP and BPEI with varied molecular weight (600, 1800, and 10,000) as the precursors. The maximum power, temperature, and pressure of the microwave instrument (Anton Paar Monowave 400) is 850 W, 300 °C, and 30 bar, respectively. The power we used to prepare NIR–II–CDs is 600 W. Specifically, 0.025 g TNP with BPEI at varied mass ratio of BPEI to TNP (20:1–1:1) were dispersed in 10 mL DI water by ultrasonication, and then the dispersions were transferred into a 10 mL microwave-dedicated reactor glass tube. The reaction temperature was varied from 80 to 230 °C and the reaction time was varied from 1 to 60 min. After a rapid microwave reaction and cooling down to room temperature naturally, obtained NIR–II–CDs were purified by filtration and dialysis to remove unreacted precursors.

NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL hybrid nanoparticles, thermosensitive liposomes (TSL) encapsulating NIR–II–CDs and indocyanine green (ICG), were synthesized through the lipid film hydration and extrusion method. Typically, HSPC, DSPE-PEG2000, cholesterol, ICG, and DPPC (molar ratio: 25:15:3:1:50) were dissolved in methanol and chloroform (1:4 ν/ν) followed by rotary evaporation at 55 °C to remove the organic solvents. The resulting thin lipid film was hydrated in (NH4)2SO4 solution (10 mL, 200 mM L−1) containing NIR–II–CDs at 55 °C for 2 h. The solution was then treated by bath sonication at 55 °C for 5 min to form a homogeneous suspension and extruded through a polycarbonate filter (100 nm) for 20 times. Finally, free ICG and NIR–II–CDs were removed by using a sephadex G-150 column.

We utilized the ammonium sulfate gradient method to encapsulate the doxorubicin (DOX) into the hydrophilic core of the NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL nanoparticles. In brief, the obtained NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL solution was dialyzed in PBS for two days to create the gradient by exchanging the exterior (NH4)2SO4. DOX at a molar ratio of 1:15 to phospholipid was incubated together with NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL for 24 h. Finally, a sephadex G-150 column was used to remove the free DOX. After separation, we used UV–vis–NIR absorption spectrum to determine the concentration of NIR–II–CDs in NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL. The entrapment efficiency (EE) and drug loading (DL) of NIR–II–CDs and DOX entrapped within the TSL was also determined. The concentration-absorbance standard equations of NIR–II–CDs and DOX were first calculated, and then the absorbance of NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL was measured. The concentration of NIR–II–CDs and DOX was calculated according to the standard equation after subtracting the absorbance of corresponding blank sample. The entrapment efficiency (EE) of NIR–II–CDs and DOX was calculated based on the following equation:

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the sample were recorded on a JEM-2100F microscope operated at 200 kV acceleration voltage using super thin carbon films. X ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with a Rigaku 18 KW D/max-2550 using Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of dried samples were collected using a Nicolet AVATAR 370 FT-IR. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The graphitic N doping levels were determined by XPS measurement and analysis. The total content of N doping was first measured by the XPS survey spectrum. The graphitic N content was then measured by deconvoluting the high-resolution N 1s spectrum and analyzed by the Avantage software. The zeta potential and size of the sample were recorded by a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 Zeta potential analyzer. UV–vis–NIR absorption spectra were obtained by Agilent Cary 5000 spectrophotometers and fluorescence spectra were carried out using a Hitachi 7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Photothermal properties of NIR–II–CDs were investigated by irradiating a centrifuge tube containing a dispersion of NIR–II–CDs (0–500 μg mL−1). NIR–II–CD solution (1 mL) was irradiated with the laser for 5 min.

We used the Fluke infrared thermal imaging camera to measure the temperature of NIR–II–CD solution every 30 s. Photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of NIR–II–CDs was calculated according to equation (1).

According to equation (3), hA can be determined. Therefore, the photothermal conversion efficiency at 808 and 1064 nm of NIR–II–CDs can be calculated to be 85.7% and 81.3%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Observation of temperature-triggered release of NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL by in vitro fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa and 4T1 cells were cultured in DMEM and RPMI 1640 supplemented with 10% FBS at 37 °C in 5% CO2/95% air atmosphere, respectively. HeLa cells (2 × 105) were seeded in culture dishes and incubated for at least 24 h. The DOX loaded NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL solution (600 μg mL−1, 100 μL) was added into the cells and examined under a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Olympus, Japan) using 488 and 561 nm laser. The fluorescence signal was collected at 500–550 and 600–700 nm, respectively. For temperature-triggered release of DOX, the HeLa cells were incubated with NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL at 42 °C for 5 min, and then the cells continued to incubate at 37 °C for another 55 min.

HeLa cells were cultured in a 96-well plate (4 × 103 cells per well). After adhering overnight, the DMEM was taken out and a fresh DMEM medium containing various concentration of NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL (31.3, 62.5, 125, 250, and 500 μg mL−1) were added. After 24 or 48 h of incubation, MTT (20 μL) was then added into the wells and incubated for another 4 h. Finally, 150 mL of DMSO was added into the cells to replace the medium. We measured the absorbance at 490 nm of each well to determine the cell viabilities via a Microplate Reader (Thermo, USA). The NIR–II–CDs, DOX, ICG and NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL were used as a control. The concentration of NIR–II–CDs is 31.3, 62.5, 125, 250, and 500 μg mL−1. The concentration of DOX is 0, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 2, and 10 μg mL−1. The concentration of ICG is 1.87, 3.75, 7.5, 15, and 30 μg mL−1. The concentration of NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL is 31.3, 62.5, 125, 250, and 500 μg mL−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro PTT and PCT in NIR-II window
ce:section.ce:para: To study the in vitro PTT efficiency of NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL and PCT efficiency of NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL, the viabilities of HeLa cells after various treatments were measured by MTT assays. As previously described, HeLa cells were cultured in a 96-well plate (4 × 103 cells per well). After adhering overnight, the DMEM was taken out and a fresh DMEM medium containing various concentration of NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL (31.3, 62.5, 125, 250, and 500 μg mL−1) were added for another 4 h incubation. Then, the HeLa cells were irradiated by the laser (1064 nm, 0.8 W cm−2) for 5 min and MTT assay was performed.

Reactive oxygen species (ROS) level was detected using 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA, Beyotime, China), which can enter cells and be hydrolyzed into a fluorescent 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescin (DCFH) probe. After seeded into 96-well plates with a density of 5000 cells per well and grown overnight, HeLa cells was cultured in the fresh medium containing NIR–II–CDs with different concentrations for 4 h. Then, the HeLa cells were irradiated by the laser (1064 nm, 0.8 W cm−2) for 5 min. After laser irradiation, HeLa cells was cultured in NIR–II–CDs with different concentrations for another 20 h. The positive controls were prepared with HeLa cells cultured in fresh medium containing Rosup for 20 min prior to the addition of DCFH-DA. Then the ROS levels were determined following the protocol, using a Microplate Reader (Thermo, USA). The ROS level is expressed as the ratio of (ODtest − ODblank)/(ODpositive − ODblank), where ODtest is the fluorescence intensity of the cells exposed to NIR–II–CDs, ODpositive is the fluorescence intensity of the cells exposed to positive control (Rosup) and ODblank is the fluorescence intensity of the wells without HeLa cells.

All of our animal experiments were conducted in compliance with the guidelines established by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Shanghai University. Female Balb/c nude mice with the age of 3–5 weeks were purchased from the SLAC Laboratory Animal Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Two million 4T1 cell suspended in PBS (0.1 mL) was subcutaneous injection into the axillary of nude mice to establish a tumor model.

An IVIS Lumina III in vivo Imaging System (PerkinElmer, USA) was used to recorded the in vivo NIR fluorescence imaging of our NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL. Mice bearing 4T1 tumors were administrated by intravenous injection with NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL (500 μg mL−1, 200 μL). The mice were anaesthetized and imaged by excitation at 700 nm and collection at 790 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo PTT and PCT in NIR-I and NIR-II window
ce:section.ce:para: Mice bearing 4T1 tumors were randomly divided into seven groups (five mice per group) and administrated by intravenous injection with different treatment only once during the treatment period (18 days): (1) saline, (2) saline + NIR-II laser (1064 nm, 0.8 W cm−2, 5 min), (3) free DOX, (4) NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL, (5) NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL (500 μg mL−1) + 808 nm laser (0.3 W cm−2, 5 min), (6) NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL (500 μg mL−1) + 1064 nm laser (0.8 W cm−2, 5 min), (7) NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL + 1064 nm laser (0.6 W cm−2, 5 min). In the NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL or NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL, the concentration of NIR–II–CDs was determined to be 500 μg mL−1. Therefore, 500 μg mL−1 refer to the concentration of NIR–II–CDs in the NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL or NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL. We recorded the volume of the tumors and body weight of the mice every other day and every day, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo deep-tissue PTT and PCT in NIR-II windows
ce:section.ce:para: 200 μL of NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL solution (500 μg mL−1) or saline were intravenously injected into the 4T1 tumor-bearing mice. Then, tumors covered by a 10 mm chicken breast tissue were exposed to 1064 nm laser (1.0 W cm−2) for 5 min. We recorded the volume of the tumors and body weight of the mice every other day and every day, respectively. For deep-tissue PTT, 100 μL of NIR–II–CD solution (750 μg mL−1) were intratumorally injected into the 4T1 tumor-bearing mice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Histology measurements, blood analysis, and hemolysis assay
ce:section.ce:para:
Major organs were collected at the end of the treatment and stained by H&E using a digital microscope (Leica QWin). For hemolysis assay, human whole blood obtained from volunteer (5 mL) was added to calcium- and magnesium-free PBS solution (10 mL). Then, RBCs were isolated from serum by centrifuging at 500 g for 10 min and repeated for five times. After washed and diluted in 50 mL PBS, the RBC suspension (0.2 mL) was added to NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL solution (0.8 mL). The final concentration of NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL solutions ranges from 100 to 500 μg mL−1. The positive control and negative control group was DI water and PBS, respectively. Percent hemolysis was calculated using the following equation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microwave synthesis and characterization of NIR–II–CDs
ce:section.ce:para: We developed an ultrafast microwave-assisted hydrothermal method for the synthesis of NIR–II–CDs using TNP and BPEI as the microwave-absorbing precursors (Fig. 1 a). We found that several synthetic parameters, including the TNP concentration, mass ratio of BPEI to TNP, hydrothermal temperature, and time, exerted an important influence on the NIR absorbance of resulting NIR–II–CDs at 1064 nm (Fig. 1b). The dramatic change in the 1064-nm NIR absorbance can be ascribed to the ultrafast reactions under microwave radiation at a power of 600 W. We believe that the ultrafast synthesis was triggered by rapid temperature rise through direct microwave energy conversion into heat by polar H2O molecules, polar amine groups in the BPEI, and the extended π electron system in the polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon, TNP. Under the optimized conditions (2.5 mg mL−1 of TNP, mBPEI: mTNP = 16:1, 230 °C, 5 min), an out-performed sample, NIR–II–CD-4.30%, was finally obtained with a high yield of 78% and further characterized by XPS, TEM, Raman, FT-IR, and other techniques.

Survey XPS analysis (Fig. 2 a) indicates that the NIR–II–CD-4.30% contains rich oxygen (14.24 at.%) and nitrogen (21.43 at.%). The oxygen-containing species is hydroxyl (-OH), as confirmed by the C 1s and O 1s spectra (Fig. 2b and d). The N 1s spectrum shows binding energy at 398.8 and 400.7 eV, corresponding to pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively (Fig. 2c). The atomic percent of pyrrolic and graphitic N were determined to be 17.13 at.% and 4.30 at.%, respectively. The presence of the hydroxyl groups and the C–N bond in the NIR–II–CDs is also revealed by FT-IR. The broad absorption band at 3300-3500 cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibrations of –OH (Fig. S1a). TEM (Fig. 2e) shows that most NIR–II–CDs (an average size: 13.1 ± 2.2 nm) are composed of several fused sp2 carbon clusters. HRTEM further displays significant structural disorder in the NIR–II–CDs (Figs. 2f and S2), which is also revealed by the weak G band compared with the D band in the Raman spectrum (Fig. S1b).

We then explored the unusual photothermal properties of black solutions (500 μg mL−1) of NIR–II–CD-4.30% in the NIR-I (700–950 nm) and NIR-II (1000–1350 nm) biological windows since the absorption spectrum well covered the wavelength range from 650 to 1300 nm (Fig. 3 a). Given the laser irradiation tolerance limit of 0.42 and 1.0 W cm−2 at 808 and 1064 nm, respectively, the photothermal effects of the NIR–II–CD-4.30% solution were evaluated under the 808 or 1064 nm laser irradiation at safety power densities of 0.1–0.4 W cm−2 and 0.4–1.0 W cm−2, respectively (Fig. 3c and d). Infrared thermographic images presented in Fig. 3b show that the temperature of the irradiated solution rapidly rose from 29.5 to 53.3 °C after continuous irradiation by the 808 nm laser (0.3 W cm−2, 5 min), while nearly the same photothermal effectiveness was observed under the 1064 nm laser (0.8 W cm−2, 5 min). The requirement of a higher power density at 1064 nm was to compensate for the weaker absorbance at 1064 nm than 808 nm (Fig. 3a).

To measure the photothermal conversion efficiency at 808 and 1064 nm (η 808 and η 1064), the changed temperature of the NIR–II–CD-4.30% solutions under irradiation was monitored to determine the T max and τ
s when irradiated for 300 s and then cooled down (Fig. 3e and f). T max and τ
s at 808 nm were 53.3 °C and 204.4 s, while T max and τs at 1064 nm were 48.0 °C and 141.8 s, respectively. On the basis of these data, ƞ 808 and η 1064 were calculated to be as high as 85.7% and 81.3%, respectively. For comparison, the photothermal conversion efficiency of representative kinds of PTT agents at the NIR-I and NIR-II windows is summarized in Table 1 . To the best of our knowledge, NIR–II–CDs have exceptionally high conversion efficiencies at 808 and 1064 nm compared with other kinds, such as NIR-I-CDs (ƞ 808: 4–58%) [30–35], graphene [36–38], carbon nanotubes (ƞ 1064: 56%) [15,16,39,40], noble metals (ƞ 808: 21–95%; ƞ 1064: 20–81%) [9,10,41,42,51,52], metal oxides (ƞ 808: 20–55%; ƞ 1064: 19–39%) [11,12,43,44,53,54], metal sulfides (ƞ 808: 24–48%; ƞ 1064: 41–67%) [13,14,45,46,55], polymers (ƞ 808: 26–71%; ƞ 1064: 28–66%) [17–19,47,48,56], and organic molecules (ƞ 808: 9–60%) [49,50]. Particularly in the NIR-II window, the efficiency of NIR–II–CDs is slightly higher than the highest value found in art-of-state metal-based PTTs (plasmonic blackbody at 1064 nm: 80.8%) [10].

We further evaluated the photothermal stability of NIR–II–CD-4.30% by laser-on and -off circles. Their recycling temperature variations were recorded under 808 or 1064 nm laser radiation for 5 min (laser on) followed by natural cooling to room temperature (laser off) for ten heating-cooling circles. No significant deterioration during the recycling was observed (Fig. 3g and h), indicating the potential of NIR–II–CDs as a durable photothermal agent for PTT applications.

To further simulate the clinical scenario, we also investigated the deep-tissue photothermal properties of our NIR–II–CD-4.30% in the NIR-I and NIR-II window. The outside surface of the vessel containing the NIR–II–CD solution (750 μg mL−1) was covered by a piece of chicken breast tissue with varied thicknesses (0–10 mm) and then irradiated at 1064 (1.0 W cm−2) or 808 nm (0.4 W cm−2), respectively (Fig. 3i and k). Compared with the 808 nm laser irradiation, the 1064 nm laser irradiation exhibited more efficient heating, which could be attributed to the lower absorption and scattering by tissues in the NIR-II window [57]. The temperature increments at irradiation tolerance limit of the NIR–II–CD solution under 1064 nm laser irradiation were 2.15-, 2.27-, 2.43-, 2.59-, and 2.75-fold of those under 808 nm laser irradiation at 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 mm tissue depths, respectively (Fig. S4). For the 1064 nm laser irradiation, there was only a 34% NIR attenuation by tissues with thickness larger than 10 mm (Fig. 3l). In contrast, for the 808 nm laser irradiation, about 77% NIR energy was lost during the penetration of only 4 mm-thickness tissue (Fig. 3j).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal effects mediated by graphitic N doping
ce:section.ce:para:
To reveal the photothermal mechanism of NIR–II–CDs as a superior PTT agent, a series of NIR–II–CD samples with different graphitic N doping levels were synthesized at 230 °C for 5 min by varying the mass ratio of BPEI to TNP (16:1, 8:1, 20:1), including NIR–II–CD-4.30% (graphitic N 4.30 at.%), NIR–II–CD-2.83% (graphitic N 2.83 at.%), and NIR–II–CD-1.68% (graphitic N 1.68 at.%). The detailed structural/composition factors and optical/photothermal characteristics of the NIR–II–CD-2.83% and NIR–II–CD-1.68% samples were measured like the NIR–II–CD-4.30% (Figs. S5–S13). Fig. 4 d lists the four structural factors of the thee typical samples, including average particle size, total N content, total O content, and graphitic N content, as well as their key photothermal parameters (T max, τ
s , and η 1064) that were measured under the same concentration (500 μg mL−1) and irradiation conditions (1064 nm, 0.6 W cm−2).

We note that NIR–II–CD-4.30% and NIR–II–CD-1.68% share nearly identical particle size (10–13 nm), total N content (∼21 at.%), and total O content (13–14 at.%), but greatly differ in NIR absorbance (Fig. 4a) and photothermal conversion (Fig. 4c), indicating that all the three parameters except for the graphitic N content are not crucial to the remarkable variation in the NIR absorbance or photothermal performance. Interestingly, under the same concentration, the absorbance of the three NIR–II–CD samples at 808 nm and 1064 nm increases with the graphitic N content in a completely linear manner (Fig. 4b). This linear relationship clearly reveals that graphitic N cores doped in the NIR–II–CDs serve as “color centers” to mediate the NIR absorption. Furthermore, the photothermal conversion efficiency at 1064 nm increases with graphitic N content: at lower levels of 1.68–2.83 at.%, the efficiency is relatively low (43.6–52.1%), but at a higher level of 4.30%, the efficiency is increased by 1.86 times (Figs. 4c and S14). This graphitic N-dependent photothermal conversion performance further displays that graphitic N centers can also serve as carrier traps for rapid conversion of absorbed NIR photons into phonons or heat.

Graphitic nitrogen centers with an excess electron have been extensively used as shallow donors to increase the carrier density in zero-band-gap graphene-based materials [58,59]. Recently, several groups have made calculations on the electronic structure of N-doped CDs and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [60,61]. They proposed that graphitic N dopants could form deep defect states within the wide band gaps by injecting excess electrons into the unoccupied p* orbitals between the HOMO and LUMO gap. However, the presence of the deep defect states in N-doped CDs as well as their correlation with graphitic N doping and PTT effects has not been supported by experiments. Herein, we further investigated the effects of graphitic N by varying the concentration of graphitic N. We first observed that the absorbance of NIR–II–CDs at 808 nm and 1064 nm increases with the graphitic N content in a completely linear manner (Fig. 4b), providing a direct evidence that the effective PTT effects in the NIR-I and NIR-II windows originate from graphitic N defects. We then hypothesized that the deep defect states between the HOMO and LUMO gap could be paramagnetic F centers induced by graphitic N defects, as shown in Fig. 4g. An excess electron donated by a graphitic N atom will be trapped near positively charged N+ centers to form a singlely occupied defect state within the wide bandgap through a large binding energy [62]. The wide band gap of NIR–II–CDs was confirmed by blue fluorescence emission at 470 nm excited at short wavelengths (370–450 nm) (Fig. 5 a). To examine the existence of singlely occupied defect states in the wide bandgap [63–65], we measured electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopies of NIR–II–CD-4.30% and NIR–II–CD-1.68% with different graphitic N contents. The g factor of NIR–II–CDs was determined to be 2.003 in both samples, which can be assigned to the paramagnetic F centers, as observed in carbon and other materials [66–68]. Moreover, the enhanced EPR signal was observed in NIR–II–CDs with a higher doping level of graphitic N (Fig. 4e). Together, we proposed a possible NIR absorption mechanism for the wide-band-gap NIR–II–CDs: doped graphitic N atoms induce F color centers to mediate optical transitions from the HOMO level to singlely occupied defect levels (F centers) and from F centers to the higher LUMO level under the NIR excitation (Fig. 4g). Owing to the presence of positively charged N+ centers at a high level, the ultrahigh-efficiency photothermal conversion is anticipated in the NIR–II–CD-4.30% sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Deep-tissue photothermal therapy in NIR-II window
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to the in vivo evaluation of NIR–II–CD-4.30% for tumor ablation, the easy internalization of NIR–II–CDs into the cytoplasm of HeLa cells incubated with NIR–II–CD-4.30% was observed by confocal imaging based on their fluorescence excited at 488 nm (Fig. 5b). The cytotoxicity assay of the NIR–II–CDs against HeLa cells was performed after incubation for 24 h or 48 h without irradiation. The cell viability of NIR–II–CDs is beyond 80% at the concentration up to 500 μg mL−1, indicating the low cytotoxicity (Fig. 5c). After the irradiation under the 1064 nm laser (0.8 W cm−2) for 5 min, the cell viability was substantially reduced owing to the photothermal effects (Fig. 5c). We also investigated the reactive oxygen species (ROS) level in the HeLa cells incubated with NIR–II–CDs under 1064 nm laser irradiation (0.8 W cm−2). No significant ROS signal was observed (Fig. S15), demonstrating that no oxidative stress was generated by the NIR–II–CDs in the presence of NIR-II laser.

We then validated the outstanding performance of the NIR–II–CDs for deep-tissue photothermal therapy in the NIR-II window. NIR–II–CDs (750 μg mL−1) were intratumorally injected into the 4T1-tumor-bearing mice and then the tumors covered by chicken breast tissue (10 mm) were exposed to 1064 (1.0 W cm−2) or 808 nm (0.4 W cm−2) laser irradiation (Fig. 5d). As presented in Fig. 5e, even in the presence of the 10 mm chicken breast tissue, complete suppression of the growth of the tumors can be observed two days after the single PTT treatment under 1064 nm laser irradiation. In contrast, no significant therapeutic overcome, compared with the control group, was observed with the same PTT agent under the 808 nm laser irradiation (Fig. 5e), though after the removal of the additional tissue, complete tumor ablation was made for the 808 nm laser irradiation even at lower power density of 0.3 W cm−2 (Fig. 7 b). Furthermore, the number of nodules metastasized to the lung was completely eliminated by the treatment of NIR–II–CDs with 1064 laser irradiation in the presence of the thick breast tissue, while the treatment of NIR–II–CD with 808 laser irradiation shows an insignificant inhibition of lung metastasis (Fig. 5f). Meanwhile, no significant body weight loss of mice was induced by the complete photothermal recession for the NIR–II–CD+1064-nm laser group (Fig. S17), indicative of the safety of all the treatments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Liposome encapsulation of NIR–II–CDs and drugs for combination therapy
ce:section.ce:para: To improve the targeting ability of the NIR–II–CDs in the tumor tissues through intravenous injection and realize the imaging-guided PTT applications, we fabricated PEGylated, multifunctional hybrid thermosensitive liposomes (TSLs), in which water-soluble NIR–II–CD as a NIR-II PTT agent was incorporated in the aqueous core and small-molecule NIR fluorescent dye ICG was intercalated in the lipid shell as a NIR imaging agent (Fig. 6a). Furthermore, we then utilized the ammonium sulfate gradient method to load small-molecule anticancer drug DOX to realize NIR light-triggered drug release for photothermal-chemo combination therapy (PCT) (Fig. 6a). The incorporation of ICG in the lipid bilayer and encapsulation of DOX and NIR–II–CDs in the hydrophilic core were confirmed by the UV–vis–NIR spectra of ICG/TSL, NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL, and NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL (Fig. 6e and S18a-c), corresponding to the distinct absorption peaks for NIR–II–CDs at ∼420 nm, for ICG at 720 and 790 nm, and for DOX at 490 nm. The final ratio of NIR–II–CDs and DOX to lipid in the TSL was determined to assess the loading efficiency of NIR–II–CDs and DOX. With 500 μg mL−1 of NIR–II–CDs and 100 μg mL−1 of DOX, the entrapment efficiency (EE) was determined to be 70.1 ± 2.6% and 90.9 ± 3.2%, respectively. TEM characterization reveals that the hybrid liposome nanoparticles, NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL, are spherical with a size of approximately 120 nm (Fig. 6b and c). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements show that the NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL and NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL hybrid nanoparticles have hydrodynamic diameter of 171.2 and 183.6 nm, respectively (Figs. 6d and S18d), being slightly larger than the TEM observation.

To assess the photothermal conversion performance of liposomal NIR–II–CD-4.30%, we measured the temperature changes of NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL solutions under 808 or 1064 nm laser irradiation (Figs. S19 and S20). With the increased concentration of NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL (100, 300, and 500 μg mL−1), the temperature of solutions increased by 8.9–25.3 °C and 11.7–23.5 °C after the 808 nm (0.3 W cm−2) and 1064 nm (0.8 W cm−2) laser irradiation, respectively (Fig. 6i). By comparing the temperature increments of NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL and NIR–II–CD-4.30%, it revealed that encapsulation into TSL will not affect the excellent photothermal conversion capability of NIR–II–CD-4.30% in the NIR region. It should be noted that the final concentration of ICG in the TSL was determined to be as low as 20 μg mL−1. At such a low concentration, ICG exhibited insignificant PTT effect under the 808 or 1064 nm laser irradiation at the low power density of 0.3 or 0.8 W cm−2, respectively. Infrared thermographic images presented in Fig. S21 reveal that no significant change of the temperature of the irradiated ICG solution (20 μg mL−1) can be observed after continuous irradiation by the 808 (0.3 W cm−2) or 1064 nm (0.8 W cm−2) laser for 5 min.

The photothermally triggered drug release ability of DOX loaded liposomal NIR–II–CD-4.30% were examined under the 1064 nm laser irradiation. The temperature of NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL solution was increased from 30 to 50 °C via accurately controlled the 1064 nm laser. Fig. 6f showed that the DOX loaded NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL retained their contents tightly below 39 °C. Afterwards, a great deal of DOX was released from liposomal system when the temperature of NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL became greater than 41 °C. We further measured the time-dependent release profile at 37 °C to confirm the structural stability of the NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL. As shown in Fig. 6g, no significant DOX release can be detected at 37 °C after 30 min of incubation. However, the extent of DOX release from NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL increased significantly in a short period of time (∼10 min) at 42 °C. We further examined the photothermally triggered drug release of NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL via in vitro cell imaging at changed temperature. Compared with the incubation of NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL at 37 °C, a bright fluorescence of DOX, which is consistent of the CLSM result of free DOX, can be observed in the cell nucleus after the temperature increase to 42 °C, indicating that the loaded DOX molecules were completely released from the TSL.

Having confirmed the photothermally triggered drug release ability, we evaluated the PTT and PCT ability of NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL on cancer cells in vitro, using a standard MTT assay. HeLa cancer cells were incubated with NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL at varied concentrations for 24 and 48 h to confirm the photothermal ablation capability. Fig. S23c revealed that with increasing the concentration, more cells incubated with NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL were killed upon the NIR-II (0.8 W cm−2) laser irradiation. In contrast, in the absence of 1064 nm laser irradiation, the control groups including the free ICG, and NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL exhibit negligible effects on the cell viability (Figs. S23a and S24b). We further inspected the combinational therapeutic ability of the DOX loaded NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL in vitro. Figs. S23b and S24a revealed that the cytotoxicity of free DOX (IC50 = 0.367 μg mL−1) was much higher than that of DOX loaded NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL, demonstrating that the DOX cannot release from the NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL without 1064 nm laser irradiation. On the contrary, the majority of HeLa cells were killed by the combination of hyperthermia and chemotherapeutics after treatment with NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL under 1064 nm laser irradiation at the ultralow power density of 0.6 W cm−2 (Fig. S23d).

We further evaluated the potential of NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL in treating tumors in vivo. The targeted efficacy and the real-time biodistribution of NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL were investigated before the in vivo PTT and PCT. After intravenously injected of NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL, the time-dependent fluorescence imaging was observed using the in vivo imaging system with a 700 nm excitation light and a 790 nm emission filter. Fig. 6j revealed that the fluorescence signals were readily observed at 12 h after injection, providing direct evidence that the PEGylated TSL was accumulated at the tumor. Furthermore, the signals lasted even for 72 h after intravenous injection, demonstrating a prolonged circulation of the PEGylated TSL in promoting their accumulation in tumor areas. For more accurate measurement, major internal organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) and tumors were removed and analyzed directly. As presented in Fig. 6k, NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL mainly accumulated in the tumor and liver, and weak signals were exhibited in the heart, spleen, lung, and kidney, indicating its selectively targeted capability in the tumor site. The quantitative analysis of targeted efficacy was shown in Fig. S25, exhibiting a significant accumulation in tumor tissue at 12–72 h post-injection.

We continued in vivo PTT and PCT on 4T1 tumor-bearing nude mice under 808 or 1064 nm laser irradiation. Mice were randomly divided into seven groups: saline group; saline plus 1064 nm laser irradiation group (0.8 W cm−2); free DOX group; NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL group; NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL plus 808 nm laser irradiation group (0.3 W cm−2); NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL plus 1064 nm laser irradiation group (0.8 W cm−2); NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL plus 1064 nm laser irradiation group (0.6 W cm−2). Fig. 6l revealed the temperature of tumor treated with NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL rapidly increased from 35.8 to 48.6 °C within 5 min upon the 1064 nm laser irradiation (0.6 W cm−2). Fig. 7b and c revealed that the tumor volume of the mice treated with saline and saline plus 1064 nm laser irradiation rapidly increased within 18 days. In contrast, the tumors in mice of NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL plus 808 or 1064 nm laser irradiation group were successfully eliminated without recurrence (Fig. 7f and g). We further investigated the PCT efficacy of DOX loaded NIR–II–CD/ICG/TSL upon 1064 nm laser irradiation (0.6 W cm−2). The tumor volume of the mice treated with free DOX and NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL without 1064 nm laser irradiation was similar to the negative control group (Fig. 7c). Impressively, a complete eradication of the tumor without reoccurrence can be observed after the PCT of NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL (1064 nm, 0.6 W cm−2). Furthermore, the number of nodules metastasized to the lung was completely eliminated by the treatment of PTT and PCT (Fig. 7d). These results strongly confirm that the NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL could targeted to the tumor and then converted the light to localized hyperthermia, inducing considerably more DOX released rapidly from the NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL to realize the maximized drug exposure in the tumor region. Therefore, the combined therapy could achieve a considerably higher therapeutic efficiency than that in either monotherapy alone.

The deep-tissue PCT efficacy in the NIR-II window (1064 nm) was further evaluated using the tumors wrapped with 10 mm of chicken breast tissue to simulation the clinical scenario. NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL solutions were intravenously injected into the 4T1 tumor-bearing mice and then the 1064 nm laser was carried out. The tumors treated with NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL were complete eradication without reoccurrence after the PCT treatment (Fig. 7f and g). Furthermore, no significant weight loss of mice can be detected for the treatment, confirming the negligible adverse effects (Fig. 7e). These results indicate that NIR–II–CD-based PCT afforded outstanding deep-tissue therapeutic effect in the NIR-II window, which is ascribed to the ultrahigh photothermal conversion efficiency of NIR–II–CDs and the delivery capacity of the liposomes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo safety evaluation of NIR–II–CD liposomal systems
ce:section.ce:para: The potential adverse effect of antitumor therapy was crucial for their development in further biomedical applications. We investigated the histopathological measurements and blood toxicity test to confirm the biosafety of liposomal NIR–II–CDs (Fig. 8 a). No meaningful changes were detected with any treatment, demonstrating that the NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL have caused no significant inflammation and infection in the treated mice. We further used the hemolysis assay to evaluate the effect of the NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL on the RBCs. No significant hemolytic behavior of the sample can be detected even the concentration up to 500 μg mL−1 (Fig. S27), confirming the excellent blood compatibility of NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL. At last, the H&E staining of major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) was performed to investigate the treatment-induced long-term toxicity. No significant long-term toxicity of all organs during the treatment period can be observed in various groups (Fig. 8b), suggesting the excellent safety of NIR–II–CD/ICG/DOX/TSL.

In summary, we have developed the rapid synthesis of NIR–II–CDs under microwave irradiation conditions using TNP and BPEI as the microwave-absorbing precursors. To reveal the direct correlation of the photothermal effects with the graphitic N doping level, NIR–II–CDs with controllable graphitic N contents were prepared by changing the precursor mass ratio. We found that the close correlation is well established by two observed effects: 1) absorbance at 808 nm and 1064 nm was consistently proportional to the graphitic N content; 2) the photothermal ƞ remarkably increased with the graphitic N content. Based on these observations and EPR measurements, we further revealed a distinct photothermal conversion mechanism in the NIR–II–CDs, where doped graphitic N sites as paramagnetic F centers can mediate the highly efficient NIR absorption and photothermal conversion. Importantly, under the optimized synthetic conditions, readily derived NIR–II–CDs with a graphitic N doping level as high as 4.30 at.% showed ultrahigh photothermal conversion efficiency of 81.3 at.% in the NIR-II window. Using NIR–II–CDs or their hybrid liposomal formula as single PTT or imaging-guided PCT agents, complete tumor ablation without recurrence was realized under the 1064-nm NIR-II laser irradiation even in the presence of the breast tissue with a thickness larger than 10 mm. Our results suggest that the photothermal properties of biocompatible CDs in the extended NIR regions can be regulated by doping a high concentration of graphitic N defects as color centers. Their integration with a biodegradable nanocarrier and a chemotherapeutic drug may find clinic applications for tumor treatment with minimized side effects.

Dengyu Pan: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Longxiang Shen: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.
